message:
perf(varint): hand-unroll the 3-9 byte read_varint tail

read_varint is the hottest decoder in the B-tree parser — every
cell header and every rowid goes through it. The 1-byte and 2-byte
fast paths were already unrolled; the 3-9 byte tail was written as
a `for (i, &byte) in buf.iter().enumerate().skip(1).take(7)` loop,
which produced an iterator chain that showed up in the Wave 5
DWARF profiles as measurable self-time inside
`CellRef::parse`. Real INSERT/seek workloads hit rowids that live
in the 3-9 byte range often enough that the iterator cost matters.

Change:

- crates/fsqlite-types/src/serial_type.rs: replace the loop with
  seven explicit blocks, one per byte position, each of which:
    * bails out with `None` if the buffer is too short (preserving
      the short-buffer contract),
    * returns `Some((value, n))` immediately if the continuation
      bit is clear,
    * otherwise shifts `value` by 7 and folds in the low 7 bits
      of the current byte before moving to the next position.
  The 9th byte retains the "use all 8 bits" encoding the SQLite
  varint format specifies.
- Behavior is bit-for-bit identical to the previous implementation
  — same short-buffer return, same parsed (value, bytes_consumed)
  tuple for every valid input. The change is pure code shape:
  the compiler now sees seven straight-line conditional returns
  instead of a bounded iterator with `.enumerate().take(7)`, which
  lets it skip the enumerator state entirely and schedule the
  length checks as predictable branches.
- The surrounding comment has been updated to point at the exact
  profile capture (`fsqlite-memmove-dwarf-213322`, 2026-04-23 MT
  8t) and to note that the hand-unrolled shape mirrors SQLite's
  own B-tree varint parser, so the intent is clear for the next
  reader who wonders why this isn't a loop.

No tests change — existing varint roundtrip / boundary tests in
this crate and the B-tree crates cover every byte-count branch.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
